name: Resheph
description: Resheph was a prominent deity in the Canaanite pantheon, associated with plague, disease, healing, and warfare. His origins are likely rooted in the ancient Near East, possibly predating the Canaanite civilization itself. He was often depicted as an archer, wielding a bow and arrows that symbolized the swift and deadly nature of disease. Resheph was not solely a bringer of destruction; he also possessed the power to avert or cure illness, suggesting a dual role as both an agent and antagonist of pestilence. He was worshipped across the Levant and into Egypt, where he was syncretized with local deities. Evidence from Ugaritic texts and archaeological finds indicates that Resheph was a significant figure in Canaanite religious life, receiving sacrifices and prayers intended to appease him and protect against outbreaks. His worship persisted for centuries, evolving and adapting as cultures interacted and beliefs shifted. Resheph's association with fire and the underworld further underscores his complex and multifaceted nature, making him a compelling figure in the study of ancient Near Eastern mythology. He was feared and respected, a powerful force to be reckoned with in a world where disease was a constant threat. His role encompassed both inflicting suffering and offering salvation, highlighting the delicate balance between life and death in the ancient worldview.
subtitle: God of Plague, Healing, and Warfare

keyMyths.title: Resheph and the Plague
keyMyths.description: Resheph unleashes a devastating plague upon the land, testing the faith of the people. Sacrifices and prayers are offered to appease him and end the suffering.

keyMyths.title: Resheph as Healer
keyMyths.description: In some narratives, Resheph is invoked as a healer, capable of curing the very diseases he inflicts. This dual nature highlights his complex role.

keyMyths.title: Resheph in Battle
keyMyths.description: Resheph accompanies the gods in battle, using his arrows to bring swift defeat to their enemies. His association with warfare emphasizes his power and ferocity.

sources.author: Mark S. Smith
sources.work: The Ugaritic Baal Cycle, Volume I: Introduction with Text, Translation and Commentary
sources.description: Provides context and translation of Ugaritic texts referencing Resheph.
